title: Wet-felted cushion cover
subtitle: A two-colour merino cover with a contrasting border and a felted-loop open-back fastening
excerpt: Wet-felt a 40 x 40 cm finished cushion cover from a 56 x 56 cm pre-shrink panel of merino roving. The base is cream merino; the border is worked in indigo or rust as a contrasting colour band around the perimeter. The back is left open with a felted-loop closure, following traditional Anatolian felted-bag practice.
sourceNotes: Observation-led; the felted-loop closure follows traditional Anatolian felted-bag practice.

glossaryTerms.definition: Wool roving that has been partially felted so it holds its shape but remains soft enough to bond with additional roving layers. Pre-felt is cut into shapes and laid on top of a base roving layer; the cut edges felt into the base during the main felting pass, producing a clean, sharp colour boundary between the two areas.

The outer boundary of a wet-felted panel. During fulling, the edges of the panel are the first area to tighten and the last to receive even agitation, so they frequently shrink at a different rate from the centre. Keeping edge fibres folded inward during early felting and checking the corners regularly reduces distortion at the tension edge.

content.text: This cushion cover is wet-felted as a single flat panel that wraps around the cushion pad with an open-back overlap, fastened by small felted loops that slip over a cord or toggle. The pre-shrink working area is 56 x 56 cm in cream merino, which shrinks 25 to 30 percent down to a 40 x 40 cm finished cover. The contrasting border in indigo or rust is introduced as strips of
pre-felt

laid on top of the base roving in the final layer pass, so the two colours bond cleanly into the main panel during felting rather than being sewn or joined afterward. The method takes roughly two to three hours of hands-on work including fulling time.

attrs.title: Slip hazard
attrs.body: Work on a non-slip surface. Hot soapy water on a smooth floor is a slip hazard; mop spills as they happen.

content.text: What you need

items.name: Merino roving, indigo or rust (contrast border)
Hot water, around 50 to 60 degrees C
Cold water, for shocking
items.qty: A second bowl or tap access

items.name: Felting mat or bubble-wrap mat (bubbles up)
Palm fitter or smooth plastic bag, to rub the surface without sticking
Large cotton towels or old bath towels, for pressing
Sharp scissors, for trimming the open-back edge and cutting the loop tabs
Short length of cotton cord or a wooden toggle, for the closure

content.text: Method
Step 1: Lay out the base panel

Lay the felting mat on a stable work surface. Pull thin, even wisps from the cream roving and lay them horizontally across a 56 x 56 cm area, working from left to right and then right to left in thin overlapping passes. Build up two horizontal layers, then two vertical layers at 90 degrees to the first. Keep the layers even: a thin patch felts faster than a thick patch, causing uneven shrinkage. You are aiming for four layers of cream across the full panel. At the edges, extend each wisp a further 5 cm beyond the 56 cm boundary; these overlapping ends will be folded back in Step 2 to give a neater
tension edge
Step 2: Add the contrast border

Lay strips of the contrast-colour roving (indigo or rust) around the perimeter of the cream panel in a band 6 to 8 cm wide, placed directly on top of the fourth cream layer. Work the contrast strips in a single horizontal and single vertical layer only. The contrast border will shrink at the same rate as the base, so it does not need the same thickness as the base panel. Fold the extended edge wisps back over the contrast border to secure the outer edge. Cover the entire panel with the felting net.
Step 3: Make the felted loops

Before wetting the main panel, prepare three small roving sausages for the loops. Pull off three 20 cm lengths of cream roving and roll each between your palms into a thin rope roughly 1 cm in diameter. These will be felted alongside the panel in Step 4 and used as button loops at the back opening. Set them aside on a damp cloth.
Step 4: Wet out and begin felting

Mix hot water and olive-oil soap in the large bowl. Drizzle the soapy water slowly over the net-covered panel, pressing down gently with a flat palm to saturate without displacing the fibres. The panel needs to be thoroughly saturated but not swimming in water; tip the mat slightly to drain any obvious pooling. Once saturated, begin rubbing through the net with flat, firm palms: 40 passes across the width, then 40 passes along the length. After 5 minutes, lift the net and check the surface. The fibres should be starting to mat and the panel should hold its shape when the net is removed. Place the three loop sausages at the edge of the mat and rub them briefly with the same soapy hands to begin their felting.
Step 5: Roll and full

Once the surface is holding together and the contrast border has bonded visibly into the cream base, move to the bamboo rolling mat. Lay the panel on the mat, roll up firmly, and roll back and forth on the table with palm pressure for 4 minutes. Unroll, rotate the panel 90 degrees, and roll again. After each rolling pass, measure the panel: it should be shrinking steadily from 56 x 56 cm toward 40 x 40 cm. Shock the panel with cold water after every two rolling passes. Cold water causes the wool fibres to contract sharply; returning to hot soapy water immediately afterward continues the felting. Repeat the hot-roll and cold-shock cycle until the panel reaches 40 x 40 cm.
Step 6: Finish the loops and attach

While the panel has reached the finished size but is still damp, roll the three loop sausages between your palms with hot soapy water until they are firm throughout. Bend each one into a loop roughly 4 cm long and press both ends together firmly. Roll the joined end between your palms to weld it. Place the three loops evenly spaced along one edge of the panel (the back opening edge), pressing the joined ends firmly against the panel's edge. Rub the attachment point with soapy fingers to bond the loop to the panel. Allow the panel to dry flat with the loops held in position by pressing them against the edge. Once dry, thread a cord or toggle through each loop to form the closure.
Step 7: Rinse and shape

Rinse the finished panel thoroughly in lukewarm water, changing the water two or three times until no soap remains. Press out excess water by rolling the panel in a clean towel and pressing firmly; do not wring. Lay the panel flat on a dry towel, pull to square on all four sides, and measure to confirm 40 x 40 cm. Dry flat at room temperature. The felt stiffens as it dries and holds the squared shape well.
Common mistakes

items.problem: The contrast border has fuzzy, indistinct edges rather than a clean line
items.cause: The contrast strips were laid too thick or not pressed down firmly enough before wetting, allowing fibres to migrate
items.fix: Use thinner wisps for the border strips and press them flat against the base layer with your palm before adding the net. If the border is already fuzzy after felting, it is permanent: plan the layout as a natural blend rather than a sharp stripe.

items.problem: The panel is 40 x 40 cm along one axis but still 45 cm along the other
items.cause: Rolling was done in only one orientation and the panel did not receive equal agitation in both directions
items.fix: Rotate 90 degrees between every rolling pass. If the panel is already at this stage, continue rolling in the short-dimension orientation only until both sides reach 40 cm.

items.problem: The felted loops have not bonded to the panel edge and lift away when the cushion is closed
items.cause: The loops were attached after the panel was fully dry, or the attachment point was not rubbed with soap to bond the fibres
items.fix: Attachment must happen while both panel and loops are still damp. Press the loop end firmly against the edge and rub with soapy fingers for 30 seconds; the fibres need to intermingle at the joint. If the cosy is already dry, dampen both surfaces with warm soapy water and repeat the rubbing.

content.text: Finishing and care

To insert the cushion pad, fold the cover around the pad with the open back edge overlapping by at least 10 cm. Thread the loops through the cord or toggle closure. Hand-wash the cover in cool water with a small amount of wool wash when needed; do not rub, do not wring, and do not machine-wash. Press out excess water in a clean towel, pull to shape, and dry flat. Re-block gently to square if the cover stretches slightly around a thick cushion pad.
